Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025

001
      FAST AND ACCURATE L ANGUAGE M ODEL D ECODING
002   VIA PARALLEL T OKEN P ROCESSING
003
004
005    Anonymous authors
006
       Paper under double-blind review
007
008
009                                                    A BSTRACT
010
011                Autoregressive decoding suffers from an inherent efficiency bottleneck due to its
012                sequential token generation process, where each token must be generated before
013                the next can be processed. This sequential dependency significantly limits the abil-
014                ity to fully exploit the parallel processing power of modern hardware. While spec-
                   ulative decoding and layer skipping offer promising speedups, both approaches
015
                   come with drawbacks. Speculative decoding relies on a secondary small “drafter”
016
                   model, which not only increases memory overhead but may also be unavailable in
017                many cases—the drafter must share the same tokenizer and vocabulary as the main
018                model for compatibility between generated and verified tokens. Layer skipping,
019                on the other hand, can cause discrepancies in the generated output compared to
020                standard autoregressive decoding, as skipped layers do not compute the key-value
021                (KV) cache that plays a crucial role in predicting future tokens. In this work, we
022                introduce a fast and accurate decoding method, ParaDecode, which accelerates
023
                   autoregressive decoding while ensuring output parity, without the need for aux-
                   iliary models or changes to original model parameters. Our approach is driven
024
                   by the observation that many tokens—particularly simple or highly-predictable
025                ones—can be accurately predicted using intermediate layer representations, with-
026                out requiring computation through the entire model. Once the model reaches a
027                certain confidence, further layers are unlikely to significantly alter the prediction.
028                ParaDecode generates tokens at an intermediate layer when confidence is suf-
029                ficiently high. This allows the next token computation to start immediately, in
030                parallel with the completion of the KV cache computation for the early-predicted
031
                   token in its remaining layers. This parallelism, implemented using batched matrix
                   operations, enables simultaneous processing of multiple tokens across different
032
                   layers, thereby maximizing hardware utilization and reducing overall decoding
033                latency. To ensure output consistency, a final verification step is applied to guar-
034                antee that the early-predicted tokens match the results of standard autoregressive
035                decoding. Experiments across diverse generation tasks, including text summariza-
036                tion, code generation, and mathematical reasoning, demonstrate that ParaDecode
037                consistently achieves superior decoding throughput compared to baselines with up
038                to 1.53× speedup, while guaranteeing output parity with standard autoregressive
039
                   decoding.1
040
041
      1       I NTRODUCTION
042
043   The autoregressive decoding process in large language models (LLMs) is increasingly becoming a
044   critical efficiency bottleneck for text generation (Khoshnoodi et al., 2024). As each token generation
045   depends on previously generated ones, the inherently sequential nature of this process severely lim-
046   its parallelization capabilities on modern hardware (Miao et al., 2023a). This challenge is becoming
047   more pressing due to two key trends. Firstly, LLMs continue to grow exponentially in size (Kaplan
048   et al., 2020; Hoffmann et al., 2022; DeepSeek, 2024), with billions or even trillions of parame-
049
      ters (Achiam et al., 2023; Zeng et al., 2023; Dubey et al., 2024; Jiang et al., 2024; Anthropic, 2024),
      resulting in substantially more time-consuming and resource-intensive computations at each step
050
      of token generation. Secondly, model-generated outputs are becoming progressively longer, driven
051   by emerging applications such as prolonged chain-of-thought reasoning (OpenAI, 2024; Snell et al.,
052   2024; Brown et al., 2024) and long-form content creation (Pham et al., 2024; Bai et al., 2024), which
053
          1
              Code is included in the submitted supplementary material.

      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025

                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   Wall-clock time

118
      Figure 2: (Left): Vanilla autoregressive decoding processes tokens strictly in sequence, limiting op-
119   portunities for parallelization. In contrast, ParaDecode starts processing the first few layers of the
120   next token in parallel with the remaining layers of the current token, once the model can confidently
121   predict the next token using intermediate LM heads. For illustration purposes, early predictions are
122   assumed to occur at layer L/2, though in practice, predictions can happen at other layers based on
123   confidence. (Right): The ideal case is illustrated in the upper part, where all tokens are predicted
124   early at shallow layers, leading to maximal parallelization. The lower diagram shows a more typi-
125   cal scenario, where tokens are early predicted at different intermediate layers, resulting in varying
126   degrees of parallelization.
127
128
      Our contributions are as follows: (1) We propose an intermediate-layer LM head training approach
      that optimizes token prediction at earlier layers and results in high-confidence early-predicted to-
129
      kens, without modifying the original model parameters; (2) We introduce a parallel token pro-
130   cessing technique that exploits batched matrix operations to concurrently process multiple tokens
131   across different model layers, significantly improving hardware utility and decoding speed; and
132   (3) ParaDecode demonstrates superior decoding throughput compared to existing methods across
133   a variety of challenging text generation tasks with up to 1.53× speedup, while maintaining output
134   consistency with standard autoregressive decoding.
135
136   2     M ETHOD : PARA D ECODE
137
138   In this section, we present our proposed method ParaDecode, as illustrated in Figure 2. The core
139   concept is to start processing the initial layers of subsequent tokens in parallel with completing the
140   current token’s layers, thereby enhancing overall decoding throughput via parallel computation. We
141   introduce the techniques for enabling early predictions using intermediate layer representations in
142   Section 2.1, followed by a detailed explanation of our parallel processing approach in Section 2.2.
143
144   2.1    L IGHTWEIGHT L ANGUAGE M ODEL H EADS E NABLE E ARLY P REDICTIONS
145
146
      Off-the-shelf LMs struggle with early predictions. Many tokens in natural language, such as
      stopwords and common phrase completions, can be easily predicted and do not require the full
147
      capacity of a model for accurate generation. However, off-the-shelf LMs typically have difficulty
148   utilizing intermediate layers for next-token prediction, as the final-layer LM head is not trained to
149   work with intermediate-layer representations. As shown in Figure 3a, applying the final-layer LM
150   head to intermediate layers results in mostly low predicted probabilities for the tokens generated
151   by the model, making early predictions with standard LMs challenging. Hence, prior research on
152   early exiting often involves designing specific model architectures or fine-tuning existing models to
153   enable intermediate-layer predictions (Elhoushi et al., 2024; Din et al., 2023; Del Corro et al., 2023).
154
      As a result, these acceleration methods typically fail to produce outputs consistent with those of the
      original off-the-shelf LMs due to changes in architecture and parameters.
155
156   Training intermediate-layer LM heads with original model parameters frozen. We hypothesize
157   that many intermediate-layer representations may already contain sufficient information for predict-
158   ing the next token, but the original LM head cannot directly harness their potential. To facilitate
159
      early predictions using intermediate-layer representations without further fine-tuning them, we in-
                                                (i)
160   troduce trainable LM heads θ (i) = {et }t∈V at each candidate early prediction layer l(i) . They take
161   the hidden representations h at layer l(i) as frozen features and predict the next word distribution
                                      (i)

      pθ(i) (t|h(i) ), which are trained to approximate the last-layer prediction p∗ (t|h∗ ) (h∗ is the last-layer

       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025

170    Figure 3: Probabilities of model-generated tokens predicted at the 8th, 16th, 24th, and 32nd (final)
171    layers of the fine-tuned Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct are shown using (a) the original last-layer language
172    model (LM) head and (b) our newly introduced lightweight LM heads. These new LM heads are
173
       trained to minimize the KL divergence loss relative to the final layer predictions, while keeping all
       original model parameters frozen. The LM heads enable close approximation of the final predictions,
174
       as seen in (b), where many tokens have confident predictions at intermediate layers, and in (c), where
175
       the KL divergence loss relative to the final layer predictions is minimal.
176
177    hidden states) by minimizing the following KL divergence loss:
178
                                         (i)
179                               exp et · h(i)
180          pθ(i) (t|h(i) ) = P                        , L(θ (i) ) = KL p∗ (t|h∗ ) pθ(i) (t|h(i) ) .
                                             (i)  (i)
181                             t′ ∈V exp et′ · h
182
183    Since we do not update h(i) , the original model parameters remain unchanged, and only the newly
184
       added LM heads are trained. As demonstrated in Figure 3c, training these intermediate-layer LM
       heads results in good approximations of the last-layer outputs, as indicated by the low KL divergence
185
       loss at the end of training. This supports our hypothesis that intermediate-layer representations
186    contain ample information for predicting the next token, and simple transformations via new LM
187    heads can effectively extract this information. Consequently, with our trained LM heads, many
188    tokens’ predicted probabilities are notably high at intermediate layers, as shown in Figure 3b.
189                                                                                                             (i)
190    Lightweight LM head implementation. The LM heads θ (i) = {et }t∈V are typically represented
191    by a weight matrix E (i) ∈ R|V|×d where d is the model dimension. Given the large vocabulary size
192
       of LMs, learning a separate LM head for each early prediction layer leads to a substantial increase in
193
       the number of parameters. Based on the observation that the weight matrix E (i) is always applied to
194
       the hidden states h(i) to compute the probability over the vocabulary pθ(i) = Softmax(h(i) E (i)⊤ ),
195    to reduce the parameter cost, we decompose it as E (i) = E ∗ T (i) where E ∗ is the last-layer LM
196    head weights, and T (i) ∈ Rd×d is a learnable transformation matrix. As d ≪ |V| for most LLMs,
197    learning T (i) is much more parameter-efficient than learning E (i) directly. We defer the proof that
198    learning T (i) retains the full expressiveness of learning E (i) to Appendix A. To further reduce the
199
       number of trainable parameters, low-rank adaptation methods (Hu et al., 2022) can be explored,
       which we leave for future work.
200
201    In practice, in an Llama3.1-8B-Instruct model (Dubey et al., 2024), each lightweight LM head T (i)
202    introduces only 16M parameters (d = 4096), while a full LM head E (i) would require 0.5B param-
203    eters (|V| = 128K). We find that a small-scale corpus with around 15K samples is sufficient to train
204    these lightweight LM heads to achieve good generalization.
205
206    2.2       PARALLEL T OKEN P ROCESSING VIA BATCHED M ATRIX O PERATIONS
207
208    Early predictions trigger parallel processing. As shown in Figure 3b, when a token’s predicted
209    probability is sufficiently high with intermediate LM heads (introduced in Section 2.1), subsequent
210    layers are unlikely to change the predictions significantly. Based on this observation, we generate
211    the next token t at layer l(i) when its probability surpasses a predefined threshold:
212
                                                   t ∼ pθ(i) (t′ |h(i) ) and               pθ(i) (t|h(i) ) > γ,                                                         (1)
213
214    where γ is a hyperparameter, and any sampling strategy can be employed (e.g., greedy or nucleus
215    sampling (Holtzman et al., 2020)). This allows parallel processing of the next token while complet-
       ing the current token’s processing. Notably, it is necessary to finish processing the remaining layers

                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    5
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


270
      distribution p∗ (t′ |h∗ ). If the two tokens do not match, we reject the early prediction:
271
272                        Reject t if t ̸= t∗ ,     where t ∼ pθ(i) (t′ |h(i) ),          t∗ ∼ p∗ (t′ |h∗ ).
273
      When an early prediction t is rejected, we halt the generation process, replace t with the actual
274
      token t∗ , and resume generation from t∗ onward. Although rejecting early predictions can lead
275   to some wasted computation, we observe in practice that this happends rarely. For example, with
276   γ = 0.8, only about 5% of all early predictions are rejected, which supports our observation that
277   high-confidence predictions from intermediate layers tend to align with the final predictions.
278
      Overall algorithm of ParaDecode is presented in Algorithm 1.
279
280   Algorithm 1: ParaDecode
281                                                                          |S|
282   Input: x = [x1 , x2 , . . . , xM ]: user prompt; S = {l(i) } i=1 : early prediction layers
283   Parameter: γ: early prediction threshold
284
      Output: Generated output sequence y = [t0 , t1 , t2 , . . . , tN ]
      KV cache ← LM(x)                                                     // initialize KV cache by processing user prompt
285
      y ← [t0 ]                                                                 // initialize output sequence with BOS token
286   P←{}                           // initialize a dictionary to track (token, layer) pairs being processed in parallel
287   P[t0 ] ← 0                                                                        // start processing BOS token at layer 0
288   while y[−1] ̸= EOS                                               // terminate generation upon generating EOS token
289    do
290       update KV cache ← LM(y; KV cache) with parallel processing of all layers in P
291       P[t] ← P[t] + 1, ∀t ∈ P                                          // proceed to the next layer for all parallel layers
292       if l(i) = P[y[−1]] ∈ S                           // if the latest token y[−1] reaches an early prediction layer
293        then
294            t ∼ pθ(i) (t′ |h(i) )                                                 // sample from the intermediate LM head
295            if pθ(i) (t|h(i) ) > γ                                              // if the probability surpasses the threshold
296             then
297
                    y ←y⊕t                                                                  // append token t to output sequence
                    P[t] ← 0                                                   // add token t at layer 0 to parallel processing
298
          if ∃t ∈ P, P[t] = L                                    // if any token being processed reaches the final layer L
299
           then
300            t∗ ∼ p∗ (t′ |h∗ )                                  // sample the gold token t∗ from final-layer predictions
301            if t∗ ∈
                     /P                                                          // verify early prediction if made previously
302             then
303                 Empty P; P[t∗ ] ← 0 // reject early prediction, halt generation, and start from t∗ at layer 0
304            remove t from P
305   return y
306
307
308   3     E XPERIMENTAL S ETUPS
309
310   3.1    E VALUATION TASKS AND BASELINES
311
312   Evaluation tasks. We evaluate our method on a diverse set of text generation tasks, including text
      summarization (i.e., XSum (Narayan et al., 2018)), code generation (i.e., HumanEval (Chen et al.,
313
      2021)), and mathematical reasoning (i.e., GSM8K (Cobbe et al., 2021)), covering a broad spectrum
314
      of language model capabilities. Due to the page limit, please refer to Appendix B for a detailed
315   introduction of the benchmarks.
316
317
      Baselines. In our work, we primarily focus on comparing with efficient decoding baselines that
      provide output parity guarantees with standard autoregressive decoding techniques, such as specula-
318
      tive decoding (Leviathan et al., 2023; Chen et al., 2023) and self-speculative decoding (Zhang et al.,
319   2024a). Despite their conceptual advantages, these methods have practical limitations. They often
320   come with inherent constraints regarding model selection or necessitate task-specific model archi-
321   tectures. Such requirements significantly compromise their practical utility and present difficulties
322   for real-world application—as we will demonstrate later (§ 4.3), they can only lead to quite limited
323   speedup or even negative speedup results compared to standard decoding unless careful hyperpa-
      rameter tuning is performed. Below, we provide a detailed introduction of the baselines.


                                                                    6
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


324   Table 1: Speedup comparison between ParaDecode and baseline decoding methods across three
325   tasks, including text summarization, code generation, and mathematical reasoning. All compared
326   methods ensure generation parity with vanilla autoregressive decoding, and the speedup results are
327   computed relative to the benchmarks established on the vanilla setup (i.e., speedup = 1× for vanilla
328   autoregressive decoding). ‘–’ represents not applicable, as there is no smaller drafter from the same
329   family of the verifier model. The best performance is highlighted in bold.
330
331                                            Text Summarization   Code Generation   Mathematical Reasoning
            Method                                   (XSum)          (HumanEval)            (GSM8K)
332
333         SpecDecode (Leviathan et al., 2023)
334           Llama3.1-8BINST
                no smaller drafter available           –                  –                     –

              CodeLlama-34BINST
336             w/ drafter CodeLlama-7BINST          1.08×              1.41×                 1.26×
337             w/ drafter CodeLlama-13BINST         1.18×              1.23×                 1.10×
338         Self-SpecDecode (Zhang et al., 2024a)
339           Llama2-7BINST                       1.05×                 1.09×                 1.10×
340           CodeLlama-13BINST                   1.03×                 1.14×                 1.12×
341           CodeLlama-34BINST                   1.07×                 1.14×                 1.14×

343           Llama3.1-8BINST                        1.15×              1.31×                 1.42×
344           CodeLlama-34BINST                      1.24×              1.45×                 1.53×
345
346
347
      SpecDecode. For this baseline, we consider two configurations: (1) CodeLlama-34B-Instruct as
      the main model (i.e., verifier) with CodeLlama-13B-Instruct as the assistant model (i.e., drafter),
348
      and (2) CodeLlama-34B-Instruct as the verifier with CodeLlama-7B-Instruct as the drafter. Note
349   that it is impractical to evaluate SpecDecode with state-of-the-art moderate-sized models such as
350   Llama3.1-8B-Instruct due to the absence of a smaller drafter model within the same family.
351
352
      Self-SpecDecode. For a fair comparison, we adopt three backbone models for Self-SpecDecode: (1)
      Llama2-7B-chat, (2) CodeLlama-13B-Instruct, and (3) CodeLlama-34B-Instruct. Following Zhang
353
      et al. (2024a), we adopt an adaptive confidence threshold strategy and set the initial threshold γ 0 =
354   0.6, the max number of draft token K = 12, and the max number of generated token T = 512. We
355   run the Bayesian optimization search for 200 iterations to determine skipped layers for configuring
356   the drafter model. We use 4 instances randomly sampled from the training set of each task for the
357   Bayesian optimization search as suggested in the original implementation. More implementation
358   details regarding training and inference can be found in Appendix C.
359
360   4     R ESULTS
361
362   4.1     M AIN RESULTS
363
364   ParaDecode consistently achieves superior inference speedup across all benchmarks. To val-
365   idate the effectiveness of our method, we compare the proposed ParaDecode with state-of-the-art
366   efficient decoding methods SpecDecode and Self-SpecDecode across a wide range of challenging
      text generation tasks. As presented in Table 1, our method consistently delivers superior speedup
367
      compared to both SpecDecode and Self-SpecDecode in both moderate size and large size models,
368
      achieving up to 1.53× speedup compared to standard autoregressive decoding.
369
370   SpecDecode (mostly) performs better when assisted with smaller models. Table 1 shows that a
371
      smaller drafter model (e.g., CodeLlama-7B-Instruct) generally leads to higher speedup for SpecDe-
      code compared to a moderate-size model (e.g., CodeLlama-13B-Instruct). This is mainly because
372
      smaller drafters have fewer parameters, requiring less computation and inference time to generate
373   draft tokens, which are then passed to a large-scale verifier (e.g., CodeLlama-34B) for parallel veri-
374   fication and correction, significantly reducing the overall time. One notable exception is in the text
375   summarization task, where using the smaller model as the drafter yields a lower speedup than the
376   moderate counterpart. We speculate that this is because smaller models like CodeLlama-7B-Instruct
377   have limited capacity to handle extremely long texts, thus producing low-quality draft tokens, which
      can lead to a higher rejection ratio during verification, thereby increasing the overall latency.

                                                              7
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


378   Table 2: Consistency ratio comparison. In principle, all compared methods in our work are expected
379   to guarantee output parity with standard autoregressive decoding as a result of the verification steps.
380   However, due to numerical precision inaccuracies and potentially tied probabilities during the com-
381   putation, the generation results might vary in practice and are subject to experimental environment
382   and hardware specifications. To empirically validate the output consistency of all methods with
383   vanilla autoregressive decoding, we report the consistency ratio for all methods to ensure a fair com-
384   parison under the same experimental environment.
385                                            Text Summarization   Code Generation    Mathematical Reasoning
386         Method                                   (XSum)          (HumanEval)             (GSM8K)
387
            SpecDecode (Leviathan et al., 2023)
388           CodeLlama-34BINST
389             w/ drafter CodeLlama-13BINST        94.98%              78.56%                93.91%
390         Self-SpecDecode (Zhang et al., 2024a)
391           Llama2-7BINST                      94.26%                 94.83%                94.14%
392           CodeLlama-13BINST                  92.53%                 95.20%                93.78%
393           CodeLlama-34BINST                  93.31%                 97.79%                94.16%
394         ParaDecode (Ours)
395           Llama3.1-8BINST                       95.36%              97.24%                95.35%
396           CodeLlama-34BINST                     93.27%              98.02%                94.65%
397
398
399
      Self-SpecDecode tends to achieve higher speedups as the model size increases. By skipping a
      larger portion of intermediate layers, Self-SpecDecode can generate draft tokens much faster than
400
      the full model, thereby achieving significant speedup. However, this approach provides limited
401   speedup improvement for mid-sized models such as Llama2-7B-Instruct. The reason is that such
402   models have only 32 layers, skipping too many layers negatively impacts the quality of the draft
403   tokens, which will lead to a high rejection rate during verification, and consequently increase the
404   decoding latency. On the contrary, skipping too few layers does not yield sufficient speedup, as the
405   performance gains stem primarily from reducing the computations associated with skipped layers.
406   ParaDecode guarantees output parity with standard autoregressive decoding. As shown in Ta-
407   ble 2, we empirically evaluate the output parity guarantee of all baseline methods across three bench-
408   marks. Despite theoretical guarantees, both SpecDecode and Self-SpecDecode fail to achieve 100%
409   generation parity with standard autoregressive decoding. One possible reason is the numerical pre-
410   cision inaccuracies during inference, as we use BF16 precision for inference, which may cause the
411   model to select different tokens compared to standard decoding.
412
413   4.2     A BLATION S TUDY
414
415   Table 3: Ablation study on ParaDecode. We report the performance of our method by ablating the
416
      training of the lightweight LM head and analyzing the impact of the verification step. The table
      shows the resulting speedup and consistency ratio on the code generation task (HumanEval).
417
418
419
                   Method                                      Consistency Ratio (%)     Speedup (×)
420                ParaDecode                                          97.24%               1.312×
421                  w/o verification step                             23.83%               1.605×
422                  w/o lightweight LM head training                  71.28%               0.863×
423
424   The verification step is essential for ensuring output parity. As shown in Table 3, As shown
425   in Table 3, removing the verification step from ParaDecode significantly reduces the consistency
426   ratio, even though it achieves a slightly higher speedup. Without verification, there is no control
427   over the generated tokens, which can lead to ParaDecode producing entirely different content as
428   early predictions may not always be reliable. This results in the acceptance of potentially incorrect
      early predicted tokens and causes significant deviations from standard autoregressive decoding. This
429
      finding underscores the importance of the verification step in our method.
430
431   Simply applying the final-layer LM head for early prediction will not lead to speedup. To vali-
      date the utility of our lightweight LM heads, we replaced the trained lightweight LM head with the

456
457   final-layer LM head for early prediction. As shown in Table 3, this ablation substantially compro-
458   mises the consistency ratio and, even worse, results in a negative speedup. This is because applying
459   the final-layer LM head to intermediate layers for early prediction only produces very low confi-
460
      dence (as illustrated in Figure 3a), which requires frequent corrections of the early predicted tokens
      as few early predictions are accepted—the resultant overheads significantly slow down the decoding
461
      process because once a token is rejected, the KV cache generated during parallel computation for
462   the next token immediately becomes invalid, as they are based on an incorrect preceding token.
463
464
465   4.3                   A NALYSIS
466
      SpecDecode is sensitive to hyper-parameter configurations. Figure 5 presents the hyperparameter
467
      study for SpecDecode, indicating that it is challenging to achieve consistent speedup without careful
468   tuning. In this study, we use CodeLlama-34B-Instruct as the backbone model, and performed a com-
469   prehensive hyperparameter search on the HumanEval benchmark, with CodeLlama-7B-Instruct and
470   CodeLlama-13B-Instruct as the assistant models, respectively. We explored various configurations
471   with different max number of draft tokens n = [2, 8, 14] and confidence threshold = [0.3, 0.5, 0.8].
472   Figure 5a and Figure 5b reveal that significant speedup variance exists across different settings.
473   Moreover, the variance becomes even larger when the confidence threshold shifts. Notably, when
474   n = 14 and the threshold is 0.3, the speedup of 13B drafter is merely 0.944, which is even worse
      than standard decoding. These findings highlight that careful tuning hyperparameters is essential to
475
      optimize SpecDec’s speedup performance and avoid potential slowdowns.
476
477   Self-SpecDecode requires task-specific architecture for optimized speedup performance. Self-
478   SpecDecode has been proposed as a training-free, inference schema that could be employed on LMs
479
      in a plug-and-play manner. However, it requires an additional Bayesian optimization (BO) process
      to first obtain a set of layers to skip (as the drafter) before it can be adopted for inference. The
480
      time-consuming BO process greatly limits its practicality, moreover, it requires a set of examples as
481   validation data to select the desired drafter. To further investigate the effect of this search process, we
482   optimize CodeLlama-34B-Instruct with different numbers of BO iterations, as shown in Figure 5c.
483   In general, more number of iterations could lead to improved evaluation performance. Yet, on
484   HumanEval, it demonstrates a decrease in performance in the process. This implies scaling the
485   number of iterations does not monotonically reflect better results, and the iteration process might
      require careful tuning to select an optimized drafter for inference.

                                                                                                             9
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


486
      ParaDecode can work well without searching thresholds for early predictions. As introduced
487   in Equation (1), the hyperparameter γ controls early predictions and triggers parallel token process-
488   ing. To study its impact, we conduct experiments with Llama3.1-8B-Instruct, and Figure 6 presents
489   the speedup results, early prediction rate, and verification rejection rate, with varying values of
490   γ = [0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1]. Specifically, γ = 1 effectively means no early predictions, as very few
491   tokens have a probability greater than 1, while γ = 0 triggers early predictions at every inference
492   step. It can be observed that the early prediction rate decreases with increasing γ, while the ver-
493
      ification rejection rate also decreases, and the inference speed is jointly affected by both factors.
      Surprisingly, we find that γ = 0 leads to the fastest overall speedup, demonstrating that our method
494
      does not require hyperparameter tuning. Based on this finding, we set γ = 0 for all experiments
495   without further tuning, and the results in Table 1 and Table 2 confirm the effectiveness of this setting.
496   We attribute this to the fine-tuned lightweight LM head, which enables early predictions with high
497   confidence, resulting in a lower verification rejection rate (around 30%, as presented in Figure 6c).
498   This finding suggests that encouraging more early predictions is beneficial for speedup and demon-
499   strates that our method can work efficiently and accurately without hyperparameter tuning, simply
500   by triggering early predictions at every step with the fine-tuned lightweight LM head.
501
502   5     R ELATED W ORK
503
504   5.1   E ARLY E XITING
505
506   Early exiting enables language models (LMs) to complete prediction at intermediate layers, re-
507   ducing computational overhead and accelerating generation. Previous approaches achieved this by
      adding decision branches or language modeling heads at various depths (Teerapittayanon et al.,
508
      2016; Huang et al., 2018a; Elbayad et al., 2020; Schuster et al., 2022). Perhaps the most similar
509
      work to ours is (Yang et al., 2024), where the authors utilize multi-processing for pipelined decod-
510   ing via early exiting. While conceptually similar, their approach diverges from ours in both method
511   design and the implementation of parallelism—they need to train the last-layer LM head for early ex-
512   iting, potentially deviating from the standard autoregressive decoding. Moreover, multi-processing
513   incurs initialization overhead and communication costs, which also limits its practical efficiency.
514   Another notable recent work is mixture-of-depth (Raposo et al., 2024), which dynamically skips
515   transformer blocks to enhance efficiency, however it still lacks the output parity guarantee with the
516
      standard autoregressive decoding. For a more detailed discussion on this line of research, we refer
      the readers to Khoshnoodi et al. (2024).
517
518
      5.2   S PECULATIVE D ECODING
519
520   Speculative decoding (Leviathan et al., 2023; Chen et al., 2023) has emerged as an effective ap-
521   proach for speeding up language model generation. It aims to reduce decoding latency by drafting
522   tokens using smaller auxiliary models and verifying multiple tokens in parallel with larger models,
523   which has been actively investigated recently (He et al., 2024; Fu et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2024a; Cai
524   et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2024b; Li et al., 2024; Qin et al., 2024). Among them, self-speculative de-
525   coding (Zhang et al., 2024a) addresses the limitation of requiring an auxiliary smaller model as the
526
      drafter. LayerSkip (Elhoushi et al., 2024) employs early exiting to generate draft tokens while con-
      tinuing with the remaining layers for verification, thereby accelerating the decoding process. Other
527
      notable works also investigate handling long text sequences (Bae et al., 2023; Kim et al., 2023;
528   Hooper et al., 2023). A comprehensive overview in this area can be found in Xia et al. (2024a).
529
530
531   6     C ONCLUSION
532
533
      In this work, we introduced ParaDecode, a new approach designed to accelerate autoregressive LM
      decoding while preserving output consistency. ParaDecode achieves this by generating the next to-
534
      kens at intermediate layers using our newly introduced lightweight LM heads. These early token
535   predictions trigger parallel token processing for the subsequent token position, utilizing batched
536   matrix operations. A verification step ensures consistency with standard autoregressive outputs.
537   Notably, ParaDecode demonstrates consistent improvements in decoding throughput across vari-
538   ous generation tasks compared to baselines, while imposing minimal constraints (e.g., no auxiliary
539   model or fine-tuning of existing model parameters required). Further discussion on limitation and
      future works is presented in Appendix D.

      R EPRODUCIBILITY S TATEMENT

      We strive to ensure the highest reproducibility of our work by providing (a) our code in the sup-
543   plementary materials, and (b) the dataset, benchmark, and implementation details in Appendices B
544   and C.

      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


756
      A    P ROOFS
757
758
759   Lemma A.1. For any E (i) ∈ R|V|×d , there exists a T (i) ∈ Rd×d such that E (i) = E ∗ T (i) .
760
761
762
763
      Proof. We first prove that E ∗ (the last-layer LM head weights) is full-rank, and then present an
764
      explicit form of T (i) that satisfies E (i) = E ∗ T (i) . During LLM pretaining, the last-layer LM
765
      head weights E ∗ are trained together with last layer hidden states H ∗ ∈ R|D|×d (|D| is the total
766   number of training tokens in the corpus) to learn the ground-truth next-token prediction probability
767   P ∗ ∈ R|D|×|V| :
768                                         P ∗ ≈ Softmax(H ∗ E ∗⊤ ).
769
      As the softmax function cannot increase matrix rank (Kanai et al., 2018), we have
770   rank(Softmax(H ∗ E ∗⊤ )) ≤ rank(H ∗ E ∗⊤ ) ≤ min{rank(E ∗ ), rank(H ∗ )} ≤ rank(E ∗ ). Thus,
771   to achieve a good approximation of P ∗ , rank(E ∗ ) must closely match rank(P ∗ ). Given the com-
772   plexity and diversity of natural language, the empirical distribution P ∗ derived from the pretraining
773   data is extremely high-rank (Yang et al., 2018). Therefore, to accurately model this high-rank dis-
774   tribution, E ∗ must be full-rank.
775   Given E ∗ being full-rank, U := E ∗⊤ E ∗ is invertible. We can define T (i) as T (i) = U −1 E ∗⊤ E (i) ,
776   then:
777                                                                              
778
                         E ∗ T (i) = E ∗ U −1 E ∗⊤ E (i) = E ∗ (E ∗⊤ E ∗ )−1 E ∗⊤  E (i)
779                                                         |         {z         }
780                                                                   =P

781   Note that P = E ∗ (E ∗⊤ E ∗ )−1 E ∗⊤ is the projection matrix onto the column space of E ∗ (as
782   P 2 = P ). Since E ∗ is full rank, E (i) lies in the column space of E ∗ . Therefore, applying P to
783   E (i) gives E (i) itself, confirming that E (i) can be expressed as E ∗ T (i) .
784
785
786
787
788   B    B ENCHMARK D ETAILS
789
790
      We evaluate our method on a diverse set of text generation tasks, including text summarization, code
791   generation, and mathematical reasoning, covering a broad spectrum of language model capabilities.
792
793   Text summarization. For text summarization, we use the widely adopted extreme summarization
794
      (XSum) dataset (Narayan et al., 2018), where the models are prompted to produce a single-sentence
      summary of a news article, testing their ability to identify and precisely summarize the most salient
795
      information in a coherent sentence. Following previous works (Zhang et al., 2024a), we randomly
796   sample 1K instances from the test split for evaluation, and 10K instances from the training split for
797   training the lightweight LM head.
798
      Code generation. For code generation, we evaluate our method on the HumanEval (Chen et al.,
799
      2021) benchmark, which assesses Python programming skills through a variety of coding problems,
800
      ranging from basic tasks to complex problem-solving challenges. Since the standard HumanEval
801   benchmark does not provide a training set, we use the entire MBPP (Austin et al., 2021) dataset
802   for training, which contains a set of crowd-sourced Python programming problems designed to
803   be solvable by entry-level programmers, covering programming fundamentals and standard library
804   functionality. This results in a total of 974 training samples and 164 test samples for this task.
805   Mathmatical reasoning. We use GSM8K (Cobbe et al., 2021) as the benchmark for mathemati-
806   cal reasoning, which contains diverse grade-school math word problems created by human problem
807   writers. The dataset consists of 7.5K training problems and 1K test problems. These problems typi-
808   cally require multiple reasoning steps to solve and involve performing a sequence of basic arithmetic
809   operations (such as addition and subtraction) to arrive at the final answer. The goal of this task is
      specifically to evaluate the LLM’s ability in multi-step mathematical reasoning.


                                                        15
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


810
      C       I MPLEMENTATION D ETAILS
811
812
      Training details. The lightweight LM heads in our method are trained through full-parameter fine-
813   tuning using the alignment-handbook repository2 with Nvidia H100 GPUs. Specifically, we utilize
814   DeepSpeed ZeRO-3 (Rajbhandari et al., 2020) along with FlashAttention (Dao, 2024) for distributed
815   training, and we enable BF16 mixed precision training to enhance training efficiency. We gener-
816   ate on-policy data to train the lightweight LM heads by prompting the off-the-shelf Llama3.1-8B-
817   Instruct or CodeLlama-34B-Instruct to produce responses using greedy decoding for prompts from
818   the mixed training split obtained from the benchmarks. By default, our models are trained using the
      Adam optimizer (Kingma & Ba, 2014) for 50 epochs, with a batch size of 128, a learning rate of
819
      5e-3, and a cosine learning rate schedule with 3% warmup steps.
820
821   Inference details. During inference, we adopt the zero-shot evaluation by directly prompting the
822   model to generate responses and apply the corresponding chat templates to format the prompts, as all
823
      backbone models used in our work are instruction-tuned versions. The framework is implemented
      using the HuggingFace Transformers library3 , and we utilize the standard greedy decoding strategy
824
      as the baseline for a reproducible comparison. Following Zhang et al. (2024a), the maximum number
825   of new tokens is set to 512.
826
827
828   D       L IMITATIONS AND F UTURE W ORK
829
830
      Limitations. Our work focuses on accelerating LM decoding without directly improving the qual-
      ity of the outputs, so ParaDecode may encounter similar issues as general LM decoding, such as hal-
831
      lucinations (Huang et al., 2023) and generating unsafe content (Zhang et al., 2024b). Additionally,
832   while ParaDecode consistently accelerates standard autoregressive decoding, it may occasionally
833   incur higher FLOPs, particularly when an early-predicted token does not match the gold token, ne-
834   cessitating a reversion. However, such cases are rare in practice, and we find that strict parity with
835   standard autoregressive decoding is not always necessary. The early-predicted tokens, even when
836   they differ from the final prediction, are typically still meaningful. Therefore, one might consider
837   relaxing strict consistency requirements to avoid unnecessary computational waste.
838
839   Future work. In the future, we plan to integrate ParaDecode with other efficiency-enhancing tech-
840
      niques like model pruning and quantization. Model pruning (Xia et al., 2024b) offer the potential
      to reduce model size and parameter space by identifying and removing less critical weights or neu-
841
      rons. When combined with ParaDecode, pruning could lead to even faster decoding times and
842   lower memory usage without significantly impacting performance. Similarly, ParaDecode can also
843   be seamlessly integrated with quantization (Dettmers et al., 2023), which reduces the precision of
844   model weights and activations, substantially lowering memory and compute requirements. These
845   directions could be particularly valuable for efficient inference on mobile and edge devices.
846

850
851
852
853
854

          3
              https://github.com/huggingface/transformers
